Design & Development of Air-Blast Atomizer

Interfacial MechanicsHigh-Speed ImagingSpray AtomizationCombustionBasilisk C

This project expands into shear-driven breakup in air-blast atomizers, focusing on optimizing spray atomization for clean combustion systems. The work directly relates to enhancing fuel efficiency and reducing emissions in the aerospace and energy industries. A custom experimental rig was designed and built, featuring a Photron high-speed camera, fiber optic illumination, Fluke airflow meter, and Digiten flowmeter. An airfoil-shaped water outlet was fabricated and tested under controlled airflow conditions to study liquid sheet breakup dynamics at the gas-liquid interface.

Key Findings

The experimental setup successfully captured high-speed imaging of shear-driven atomization. The air-blast geometry enabled controlled study of ligament formation and droplet generation, providing data relevant to combustion efficiency and emissions reduction.

Abrasive Water Jet Cutting Machine

Creo CADANSYS FluentANSYS StructuralCNCArduinoFabrication

The main aim of this project was to make a prototype of an industrially applicable low-pressure water jet cutting machine that can be numerically controlled in 2 axes and used for cutting softer materials. The design was done in Creo Parametric, and components were validated in ANSYS Fluent (CFD for nozzle force — pressures up to 4.8 × 10⁷ Pa) and ANSYS Structural before manufacturing the final assembly. The system comprises a plunger pump, Arduino microcontroller, three stepper motors, rolling wheels, a water accumulator with drain, and a precision 0.5 mm nozzle holder — all assembled on a rigid main frame.

Key Findings

The machine was successfully manufactured with proper design and materials, achieving 2-D motion along the top beam via Arduino microcontroller. CFD results confirmed adequate nozzle force for cutting softer materials at low operating pressures.

V-10 Engine Design & Analysis

SolidWorksCreoThermal FEAStructural FEAANSYS

The main aim of this project was to design and perform full analysis on a V-10 engine. Both thermal and structural analyses were carried out. The design was done in Creo with select parts modeled in SolidWorks, and components were validated in ANSYS Fluent (thermal) and ANSYS Structural before the final presentation. The engine assembly includes crankshaft, camshaft, pistons, connecting rods, fuel rail, oil pan, timing pulleys, valves (2 inlet + 2 exhaust per cylinder), fuel injectors, and flywheel — with complete sub-assembly drawings and a 31-item bill of materials.

Key Findings

Thermal analysis in ANSYS Fluent identified high-stress regions in the engine block and cylinder head. Engine thermal efficiency was also determined. The maximum stress data enables more informed material selection for each component.

Hazardous Chemical Ventilation Analysis

ANSYS FluentParticle TrackingVentilation CFDCreoIndoor Air Quality

The main aim of this project was to determine how long it would take for benzene or any harmful chemical to leave a room through the top ventilation system. The room was designed in Creo Parametric and meshed in ANSYS. Steady and transient particle-tracking simulations were performed in ANSYS Fluent to trace the pathlines of harmful particles colored by velocity magnitude (up to 7.85 m/s). Particle residence time and whether contaminants could reach an occupant's breathing zone were also determined.

Key Findings

A proper ventilation system can be designed from CFD data, and the best outlet locations for harmful chemicals can be identified. The simulation confirmed which configurations minimized occupant exposure time and guided optimal placement of air outlets.

Non-Premixed Turbulent Flame Simulation

Combustion ModelingTurbulenceANSYS FluentSwirl DynamicsNon-premixed Flame

The main aim of this project was to model a combustion chamber in both 2-D and 3-D in ANSYS Fluent, determining the length at which combustion takes place, increasing combustion efficiency, and analyzing swirl effects. Non-premixed turbulent flame models were applied to simulate the reacting flow. Particle residence time was tracked under both reacting and non-reacting conditions. Swirl was introduced in the 3-D model to study air-fuel mixing.

Key Findings

The combustor length was sufficient for complete combustion, but angular velocity was insufficient for proper swirl-induced mixing. Particle residence time was much lower without combustion as particles exited directly. Modifying chamber dimensions, reducing inlet velocity, and using more readily ignitable fuel compositions would improve thermal efficiency and reduce fuel wastage.

Flow Through Porous Media — Catalytic Converter

Porous MediaANSYS FluentExhaust SystemsPressure DropCatalytic Converter

By modeling the exhaust gas flow, pressure drop, and uniformity of flow through the catalytic converter in ANSYS Fluent, a substrate can be determined that has the best porosity and features for gas flow. Maximum total kinetic energy was also determined. Velocity vector plots and pressure contours on the central plane were used to characterize flow behavior through the monolith substrate under varying mass flow rates.

Key Findings

Pressure drop across the substrate increases with mass flow rate. Flow distribution within the monolith depends strongly on diffuser performance, which is a complex function of Reynolds number, brick resistance, and inlet pipe length and bending angles. These results guide substrate porosity selection and diffuser geometry optimization.
